speaker: SS
words: I'll start by asking you how your father wound up coming out

speaker: SS
words: here.

speaker: EM
words: He was sent here with the Weyerhaeuser Lumber Company- Potlatch

speaker: EM
words: Forest was the same as Weyerhaeuser then, see. He was sent out

speaker: EM
words: here from the East, to grade lumber. See, there was several of

speaker: EM
words: 'em sent out here from the East, Wisconsin, all over to work in

speaker: EM
words: this mill when they first started here. That's where all of your

speaker: EM
words: lumbermen come from was from back East in Wisconsin and Michigan

speaker: EM
words: and that.

speaker: SS
words: You mean your father was working for them back there?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. Yeah, he worked for Weyerhaeuser back there. Of course,

speaker: EM
words: those were just little mills then back there in that time.

speaker: SS
words: Did he care much about the idea of coming out here?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah, yeah, he wanted to come out here. I guess, I don't

speaker: EM
words: know, I was only five years old then. He musta wanted to come

speaker: EM
words: out here or he wouldn't a come.

speaker: SS
words: What was that town in Wisconsin?

speaker: EM
words: I don't know just exactly what it is-from different places,

speaker: EM
words: Marinette, Michigan and Menominee, Michigan and different places

speaker: EM
words: there you know. Saxon, Wisconsin, all little mills, you know,

speaker: EM
words: all around, you move from one place to another.

speaker: SS
words: Had he followed that lumber grading right along?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. That's all I've done ever since I come here. Fortyfour

speaker: EM
words: years now, here.

speaker: SS
words: You followed in his footsteps?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. Well, there's nothing else to do here in this town then.

speaker: SS
words: Just lumber business, that's all there is here. Of course, when

speaker: SS
words: I quit school in the eighth grade and I went to work down here.

speaker: SS
words: Us kids all during the summer months, we'd work here in the mill —

speaker: SS
words: cut thistles and stuff like that around the yard, you know. Had

speaker: SS
words: a long handled rig with sharp point, blade just about like that

speaker: SS
words: used to go along and dig these dandelions out, out of the yard

speaker: SS
words: and every place else.

speaker: SS
words: Is that the first time you worked for them?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What did they pay the kids for doing this?

speaker: SS
words: Heck, I've loaded lumber out here in the yard for ten cents an

speaker: SS
words: hour, ten hours a day. Ten cents an hour for ten hours. People

speaker: SS
words: don't believe that, but, by golly, that's what they were paying

speaker: SS
words: then.

speaker: SS
words: Was that for-

speaker: SS
words: I worked for twelve and a half cents an hour in the mill edging

speaker: SS
words: in the mill for twelve and a half cents an hour. Eight hours

speaker: SS
words: a day, sometimes ten hours.

speaker: SS
words: What about back in the midwest, do you know what your father used

speaker: SS
words: to make when he was working back there?

speaker: SS
words: No, not very much. Heck, when they was only paying about ten

speaker: SS
words: cents here they wasn't paying very much back there then, cause

speaker: SS
words: wages never were in the East like they are out in the West. He

speaker: SS
words: made—I imagine he made around thirty dollars a month, maybe a

speaker: SS
words: little more than that. Probably lucky to get

speaker: SS
words: Had your father graded 3umbef-is that what heM done for

speaker: SS
words: working life? Or did he do other work, too?

speaker: SS
words: No, that was all. That's all he knew. Of course, well, he wor

speaker: SS
words: ked in abrewery back East for awhile before-but Idon't know,

speaker: SS
words: Ithink that was in Chicago where he worked there in abrewery.

speaker: SS
words: Was he born in-

speaker: SS
words: -in Germany.

speaker: SS
words: He was born in Germany.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know about when he carae over-how old he was, Imea„- was

speaker: SS
words: he still-

speaker: SS
words: No, Idon't. No, Idon't know, my sister might know but Idon't

speaker: SS
words: know.

speaker: SS
words: It doesn't matter. Iunderstand that they had pretty much cut

speaker: SS
words: out in the mid„est-Weyerhaeuser-by the time he'd come to Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know if that's the truth?

speaker: SS
words: Cutout-what do you mean?

speaker: SS
words: Well.they'd already  most of the timber, the big timber there?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, heck, no. Just started in here. First mill they had was

speaker: SS
words: down here in Palouse.

speaker: SS
words: I meant in the midwest-not here-I hear near that they were just star

speaker: SS
words: ting here.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yes. then they kept moving out and they come out in the West.

speaker: SS
words: where it really started right the middle-Montana, North Dakota that's more farming,

speaker: SS
words: cattle

speaker: SS
words: country and your lumber business is out here.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember coming out here when you were just a kid?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. Yeah, we come here-come to Palouse from Spokane-got to

speaker: EM
words: Spokane then come down on the train to Palouse and they had the

speaker: EM
words: train here and we come on here on the tenth of May-come here.

speaker: EM
words: Got down here-come in here about noon, twelve o'clock from Pa

speaker: EM
words: louse down here to the depot. And raining, boy, it was a raining!

speaker: EM
words: Course, no roads, old mud roads, you know and we walked those

speaker: EM
words: mud- course we had those old shoes on, button shoes, then and

speaker: EM
words: wading mud all the way from that depot clear back up here-those

speaker: EM
words: trees back of where we used to live. All that old mud road, pa

speaker: EM
words: cking suitcases in that rain. No raincoats or anything! The

speaker: EM
words: clothes we had on, boy, we were soaked when we come up here.

speaker: SS
words: Did you bring much with you from the midwest?

speaker: EM
words: No, just a suitcase is all. No, my dad'd already been here and

speaker: EM
words: he'd got stuff, you know, for the house.

speaker: SS
words: He came out first and got settled?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. Yeah, that mill, they put that up here in 1906-is when

speaker: EM
words: they moved the mill up here and we came out here in 1908. Nah,

speaker: EM
words: there wasn't much of a town here then. Of course, they showed

speaker: EM
words: pictures of it-like the whole town is right now-said the whole

speaker: EM
words: town was already here, I don't know where they got the pictures.

speaker: EM
words: They claim that was in 1902. Well, the town wasn't even here in

speaker: EM
words: 1902. (Chuckles) I don't where anybody got that idea. Somebody

speaker: EM
words: had a picture here on the calender that the school puts outhad

speaker: EM
words: a picture of the town, said it was taken in 1902-well, I

speaker: EM
words: know a darn sight better, because it wasn't here in 1902! They

speaker: EM
words: couldn't very well build a mill here in 1902 when there was no

speaker: EM
words: body living here.

speaker: SS
words: What was the idea of a company town? Did they just build the

speaker: SS
words: whole thing up?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah, they built these housedand then rented them out, see

speaker: EM
words: to people that work here. Yeah, they rented the houses out.

speaker: EM
words: They probably cost-these houses-paid a couple hundred dollars

speaker: EM
words: for this house here, then you go payin' ten or twenty dollars

speaker: EM
words: a month for it, didn't take long to pay for it.(Chuckles) And

speaker: EM
words: did that for years and years. So, you can just see over sixty

speaker: EM
words: years, they paid twenty dollars a month for this house for six

speaker: EM
words: ty years!

speaker: SS
words: You think the company made money on this sort of thing?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, they had to make money! Figure it out for yourself; sixty

speaker: EM
words: years at twenty dollars a month how much that amounts to and it

speaker: EM
words: cost them about $200 to build it at that time.

speaker: SS
words: What about the maintenance?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, they had to take care of it, they took care of all- of

speaker: EM
words: course these houses weren't modern; none of them modem. All

speaker: EM
words: outside toilets and everything. Of course, the company, they

speaker: EM
words: put on this tar paper roofing then shingles, stuff like that.

speaker: EM
words: And they'd paint the houses and paper inside the house and that.

speaker: EM
words: Wallpaper it. They did that. But that's all they did do. They

speaker: EM
words: had to do something to keep it up, their houses, and they

speaker: EM
words: couldn't very well ask the renters to do it. No, if you wanted

speaker: EM
words: your house papered inside or anything, you'd just go to the of

speaker: EM
words: fice and tell 'em and they'd come up and paper it for you. Pick

speaker: EM
words: out any kind of paper you wanted, wallpaper. And they didn't

speaker: EM
words: have electric lights either, had lamps. Then finally they put

speaker: EM
words: in electric lights.

speaker: SS
words: Well, why would they want to own it all themselves and have to

speaker: SS
words: keep it up instead of having-just let people buy them?

speaker: EM
words: Well, they was making money off of these houses; wasn't costing

speaker: EM
words: them anything, just for material, paper and stuff like that J

speaker: EM
words: you was paying for that in the rent. Naw, they didn't lose any

speaker: EM
words: thing on it.

speaker: SS
words: Did you start going to school as soon as you got here?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, I started to school when I was six years old here and I went

speaker: EM
words: through the eighth grade and I quit when I was in the eighth and

speaker: EM
words: went to work. I didn't like school!(Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: I've heard the school here was better than most places.

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yes, yeah, there's been more kids graduated from this school

speaker: EM
words: here and got into better jobs than any other school around the

speaker: EM
words: country. They've always been good teachers here and everything

speaker: EM
words: here. Well-can't think of his name now- was from Potlatch. He

speaker: EM
words: graduated from here, he's one that designed that Lewiston Hill.

speaker: EM
words: Oh, there's several of 'em here had good jobs. Most kids around

speaker: EM
words: here that had an education^ they went out and did pretty good so

speaker: EM
words: far. Oh, I don't know, Potlatch seems they put more money into

speaker: EM
words: the school here than any other school. Better teachers and every

speaker: EM
words: thing. They pay more in here than they do in Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: Was that the company in the early days that was running the school?

speaker: EM
words: No, I don't know whether it was the company,no, I don't think it

speaker: EM
words: was, it must have been state or somebody because the company didn't

speaker: EM
words: keep it up. Of course, we didn't have to pay for our books or

speaker: EM
words: anything, all we had to pay for was pencils and paper and stuff

speaker: EM
words: like that. Our books were all furnished.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you then-you were about thirteen or fourteen when

speaker: SS
words: you started working?

speaker: EM
words: Well, let's see-I started in-six and eight- about fourteen-fifteen-

speaker: EM
words: sixteen years old when I started to work.

speaker: SS
words: Was it hard to get hired by then?

speaker: EM
words: No, all you had to do was during the summer go down and ask 'em

speaker: EM
words: for a job and they'd give it to you. Always had something doing:

speaker: EM
words: lot of 'em, of course, worked for farmers and everything else,

speaker: EM
words: you know. Go out in the summertime and pick potatoes. Farmers

speaker: EM
words: they'd come along with a plow and plow the spuds up and we'd

speaker: EM
words: pick 'em for ten cents a sack!(Chuckles) Boy, you wouldn't catch

speaker: EM
words: anybody doing that now! Go out in the field and pick spuds for

speaker: EM
words: ten cents a sack!

speaker: SS
words: So, what was the first work that you did after this^ pulling weeds

speaker: SS
words: for 'em?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, went out in the yard, workin out there and picking up lum

speaker: EM
words: ber-pieces of lumber and cleaning up and then loading lumber on

speaker: EM
words: trucks from the pile, put 'em on these four-wheel trucks that

speaker: EM
words: they had. One man'd be up on the pile and he'd hand the lumber

speaker: EM
words: down to the guy on the truck and he'd build it up and send the

speaker: EM
words: load. Those loads go to the planer, see. They surfaced the lum

speaker: EM
words: ber any everything there. Get way up on those high piles and

speaker: EM
words: reach way-sometimes you'd have to get on the pile and you'd have

speaker: EM
words: to take two boards and hold your fingers this way then clear over

speaker: EM
words: to the edge of the pile so the guy below could reach it. Yeah,

speaker: EM
words: then Iwent into the sawmill. Worked in there picking edgings

speaker: EM
words: and helping edgermen and then edged and then Irode carriage.

speaker: EM
words: Then Iworked in the lath mill; then Iworked in the firehold

speaker: EM
words: during the Depression and then went to the planer. Worked over

speaker: EM
words: there in the manufacturing plant. Then they did away with that

speaker: EM
words: and Iwent back to the planer again and fed machine there. Then

speaker: EM
words: I run a resaw. Oh, I did about every kind of work there was around

speaker: EM
words: here in the mill.

speaker: EM
words: How come you went from one kind of work to another? I thought a

speaker: EM
words: lot of guys-

speaker: EM
words: You're always looking for abetter job, that paid more, that's

speaker: EM
words: why. Yeah, well, wherever you can get the most money's the job

speaker: EM
words: you was looking for. Because, heck if you was working for, say,

speaker: EM
words: eighty cents an hour on one job and you got a chance to, say,

speaker: EM
words: eighty-seven and ahalf cents an hour, naturally you're going to

speaker: EM
words: try to get that job there. You just ask for ajob, see, and then

speaker: EM
words: if they'd see that you wanted acertain job, maybe that guy that

speaker: EM
words: had the job you wanted-they needed him on some other job, see,

speaker: EM
words: and they'd transfer him and then you'd get that job. Just put

speaker: EM
words: your application in for a certain job, you know,-

speaker: EM
words: Did you go to a foreman and ask him-

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. Yeah, you could do anything you wanted and get a job then,

speaker: EM
words: but, boy, now, why, you take what you can get. If they want to

speaker: EM
words: put you on ajob and you don't want to go, you're just out of a

speaker: EM
words: job, that's all.

speaker: SS
words: Wasn't that way then?

speaker: EM
words: No, uh-huh. No, I quit the sawmill. Boss that they had there

speaker: EM
words: and I didn't get agreed and I quit, went over to the planer

speaker: EM
words: and asked the general manager over there for a job,' says,"Yeah,

speaker: EM
words: you're just the man," he was looking for. Sent me over to the

speaker: EM
words: replant there, that's all there was to it. I quit at noon and

speaker: EM
words: went to work that noon on the other job.

speaker: SS
words: What made you decide to quit?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, Icouldn't get along with that boss down there. He'd come

speaker: EM
words: along and he didn't like me and I didn't like him and I was ed

speaker: EM
words: ging in the mill and he come over there and he says, "Let me

speaker: EM
words: have your apron." I had a leather apron on; Ihanded him the

speaker: EM
words: apron and didn't say anything more and I went at noon- why, I

speaker: EM
words: just walked over and told the assistant boss there that he had,

speaker: EM
words: told him, I said, "You'd better get another edgerman." Says "I'm

speaker: EM
words: through." That's all there was to it. Well, during the war,why

speaker: EM
words: heck they had a lot of women working down there, too. Wife she

speaker: EM
words: worked down there.

speaker: SS
words: Which war?

speaker: EM
words: Second World War. Oh, she worked down there, too; lot of women

speaker: EM
words: working in there. In the remanufacturing plant where I wor

speaker: EM
words: ked, there was pretty near all women working in there except the

speaker: EM
words: graders and the guys running the trimmer and myself. The rest

speaker: EM
words: of 'em were all women, pulling lumber and stuff in there.

words: Was it hard work for them to do?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, bet your life, when you get those 2x12's-red fir) 2x12 s,

speaker: EM
words: sixteen and eighteen feet long and you pull one of those off the

speaker: EM
words: chain, that was pretty hard work for a woman to do. But %did

speaker: EM
words: her. A lot of times I'd go over there and help, when I didn't

speaker: EM
words: have nothing to do, I'd help 'em pull lumber. But they had to

speaker: EM
words: do it, if they didn't do it, why, they was out of a job.

speaker: SS
words: Well, take like this boss that you didn't get along with; I've

speaker: SS
words: heard that some of the bosses were not-were really pretty bad

speaker: SS
words: to work for. What was the matter with this boss, I mean, what

speaker: SS
words: was it that made him a bad man to work for?

speaker: EM
words: Ch, I don't know. He was a guy-he was a barber in the first place,

speaker: EM
words: never knew anything about sawmills and he got in with the general

speaker: EM
words: manager here and the general manager give him a job bossing down

speaker: EM
words: at Elk River there. So, he worked up hereA for a little while up here

speaker: EM
words: in the sawmill sawing and I don't know how he got in with the

speaker: EM
words: general manager, but he got in anyway, got to be a boss down

speaker: EM
words: there. The guy that was boss in there, he used to-my mother

speaker: EM
words: lived down there and she had a few boarders, just guys come in

speaker: EM
words: and board, you know, eating their meals and one of these boar

speaker: EM
words: ders was the boss then, so when this other guy come in and took

speaker: EM
words: his job, why then that made me mad, too, and that's where we

speaker: EM
words: didn't get along.

speaker: SS
words: Well, to get to be a boss, did you have to have an 'in' or did

speaker: SS
words: you do that-

speaker: EM
words: Oh, the company had general manager and superintendents and

speaker: EM
words: that and they were the ones that picked their bosses. Because

speaker: EM
words: those days you had to know your lumber business or something,

speaker: EM
words: you know, to get in. But they had a lot of bosses-now, why,

speaker: EM
words: heck, the kids that come out of school up here, high school,

speaker: EM
words: go down here and ask for a job and work there a couple of weeks

speaker: EM
words: they put 'em as graders and bosses and everything else down there

speaker: EM
words: now! They don't have anybody working in there any more in the

speaker: EM
words: lumber business. They're all retired. All these kids-I watched

speaker: EM
words: em several years ago at the planer, kids grading down thereand

speaker: EM
words: they wasn't grading lumber, they'd see a board come along

speaker: EM
words: and put a mark on it; pick up a knot and throw it at some other

speaker: EM
words: kid down there and(chuckles) oh, they get by with anything down

speaker: EM
words: there now.

speaker: SS
words: Did they watch pretty closely when you worked in the old days?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, you bet your life! You had to know what you were doing, if

speaker: EM
words: you didn't know what you were doing, why, you didn't get a job.

speaker: EM
words: You didn't fool around, play around, then. Some boss seen you

speaker: EM
words: fooling around, wasn't doing anything, just playing around, why,

speaker: EM
words: you got canned right now. Naw, these kids now, these guys down

speaker: EM
words: there now, they get by with murder. Of course, some of the bosses

speaker: EM
words: they got down there don't know any more than the kids that are

speaker: EM
words: starting in. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Did the bosses really know their stuff then?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yes, they had to. Yeah, they had to know.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard about this 'easy hire, easy fire'-easy to get on and

speaker: SS
words: the bosses could can you for almost anything they wanted to.

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. If you was in good with the boss, why, you was sittin'

speaker: EM
words: pretty then. But very few of them-once you get in with them.

speaker: EM
words: The most of these guys that they get as job bosses now, are just

speaker: EM
words: suckers for the company, that's what they are. Guys that run to

speaker: EM
words: the boss or something, squeal or. somebody else or something. Just

speaker: EM
words: like one guy here, young fellow, working down here and he was a

speaker: EM
words: job steward for the union. Every night he'd run up to the of

speaker: EM
words: fice and tell 'era what the union was going to do and everything

speaker: EM
words: else. Go to the meetings and tell 'em, see.

speaker: SS
words: This is now?

speaker: EM
words: No, he did that. And, by golly, he did that and finally they put

speaker: EM
words: him in as a boss down here. He's still a boss down there yet.

speaker: EM
words: They kicked him out of the union. Yeah, he'd run up and tell

speaker: EM
words: the guys in the office all the time what was going on, union

speaker: EM
words: meetings and everything.

speaker: SS
words: Well, didn't they go for an awful long time with no union there?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, there was a long time. They had what they cal

speaker: EM
words: led International Workers of America and then there come inthe

speaker: EM
words: AF of L-come in. First the IWW's and then this other and

speaker: EM
words: then the AF of L and CIO. Well, you didn't have to belong to

speaker: EM
words: the union then if you didn't want to, but now after you're down

speaker: EM
words: there and work thirty days, I guess you have to sign up for the union

speaker: EM
words: now.

words: What were the conditions like when you started working in there

speaker: SS
words: in the mill? What were the conditions-the working conditions

speaker: SS
words: like?

speaker: EM
words: In what way do you mean?

speaker: SS
words: Well, you said it was a ten-hour day, instead of an eight. And

speaker: SS
words: what about breaks and stuff like that? What about even your

speaker: SS
words: meals?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, well, they had their regular hours like start in at seven

speaker: EM
words: o'clock and then at noon-had your hour at noon and then til four

speaker: EM
words: o'clock. And then from one o'clock til four o'clock. You didn't

speaker: EM
words: get no ten-minute break or anything like they do now. You had

speaker: EM
words: to work straight through-say you wanted a smoke, you'd get some

speaker: EM
words: guy to take your place and say that you was going to the toilet.

speaker: EM
words: When you did that, why, you'd go down to the toilet and smoke,

speaker: EM
words: see. That's the way the break that you'd get.

speaker: SS
words: You wasn't supposed to be smoking there?

speaker: EM
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: —You got somebody coming to visit, who's this.

speaker: EM
words: Oh, Dewey McCain. He's an old-timer here, too.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, he's getting his paper.

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. When we get through with the paper we let him have it and

speaker: EM
words: then he gets the Review and brings it down here to us, see. That's

speaker: EM
words: the Lewiston paper. He usually goes down every morning and gets

speaker: EM
words: the mail and he gets his mail then he brings us ours. He lives

speaker: EM
words: up on the corner and he owns the next two houses here. Yeah,

speaker: EM
words: him and I used to box together years ago.

speaker: SS
words: Box?

speaker: EM
words: Uh-huh. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: You mean-

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. Oh, heck, that's been over forty years ago. Yeah, him and

speaker: EM
words: I both-and we had one match here down to Palcuse-cwo brothers

speaker: EM
words: had come from Alberta, Canada and Dewey fought one of 'em and I

speaker: EM
words: fought the other one down there at Palouse and we got five dol

speaker: EM
words: lars apiece for it!(Chuckles) Where they get thousands of dol

speaker: EM
words: lars now and we had to fight three rounds for five dollars!

speaker: SS
words: Each had to go three rounds?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah.

speaker: EM
words: END OF SIDE A

speaker: EM
words: Did you go three rounds?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. I busted my thumb there when I fought this guy. First

speaker: EM

words: He was going to push 'em through the edger and I jumped over

speaker: SS
words: there and grabbed ahold of him and pulled him off.(Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Did he know what he was doing?

speaker: SS
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: Didn't he know what he was doing?

speaker: SS
words: Old George? Sure, he knew, whether he was trying to scare the

speaker: SS
words: Japs or what, or whether he really meant it, I don't know. I

speaker: SS
words: just happened to be right on the side about from here to the wall

speaker: SS
words: over there, nearer than that-nearer the chair and I just jumped

speaker: SS
words: right over a table there-rolls- and grabbed ahold of him.

speaker: SS
words: Old George, he come almost getting canned over that, him and

speaker: SS
words: his brother both.

speaker: SS
words: What about the Japanese guy? He must have been scared.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, they was scairt to death.

speaker: SS
words: Did he do anything at all to cause that?

speaker: SS
words: No, George and his brother just had it in for them, you know.

speaker: SS
words: They just didn't like the Japs. That was before the war broke

speaker: SS
words: out. Of course, after that, why, that's when-they made the Japs

speaker: SS
words: leave then, they all had to quit then.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever get to know any of 'em-any of the Japanese that were

speaker: SS
words: here working in the mill?

speaker: EM
words: No, just those- no, I never had anything to do with 'em. No,

speaker: EM
words: after the war broke out-course, these two that they left and

speaker: EM
words: went back to Japan and joined the army over there, of course.

speaker: EM
words: I asked different ones there, I knew a couple of 'em, they was

speaker: EM
words: up there in Spokane and I asked 'em about 'em and they said they'd

speaker: EM
words: went back to Japan. They didn't know whether they was killed in

speaker: EM
words: the war over there or not.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever go in their boardinghouse down here?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, I was trying to sell rain coats and shoes and

speaker: EM
words: stuff on the sideline, you know, and I'd go down there and they'd

speaker: EM
words: order stuff from me. Yeah, they didn't bother anything. 'Course,

speaker: EM
words: there only a couple of 'em that could talk any broken English

speaker: EM
words: anyway, you know, you couldn't-they couldn't understand you, you'd

speaker: EM
words: have to show 'em the stuff and explain to 'em and we'd show 'em

speaker: EM
words: the shoes or rain coat and ask 'em what size they wore and stuff

speaker: EM
words: like that. I'd go down there and they'd all order stuff from me

speaker: EM
words: a lot of 'em, and get the stuff and bring it back down there to

speaker: EM
words: 'em and they'd pay me for it. They didn't argue and say, "Well,

speaker: EM
words: that ain't what I ordered or this and that." They'd take it and

speaker: EM
words: say nothing. I did the same thing with the Greeks and Italians.

speaker: EM
words: Sell them stuff, too, that way. Make a couple dollars on the

speaker: EM
words: side.

speaker: SS
words: Did you notice much differences between the Greeks and Italians?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah, they never mixed very much together. However, the

speaker: EM
words: Greeks they'd stay on their side of the street over there-two

speaker: EM
words: rows of houses there and the Greeks'd be on this side and the

speaker: EM
words: Italians'd live on the other side. Some of 'em down at the

speaker: EM
words: other end of town. They didn't mix together very much. 'Course,

speaker: EM
words: they all worked together down to the mill and that, you know.

speaker: EM
words: (Doggone wind-yesterday was hot over at Lewiston-was it hot over

speaker: EM
words: to Moscow yesterday? Around 90 and I was sitting out here on

speaker: EM
words: the front porch all day. And this wind, I can't take that wind

speaker: EM
words: out there.)

speaker: SS
words: Did you think in those early days that there was—that the Greeks

speaker: SS
words: and Italians were very different types of people-from each other?

speaker: SS
words: Or do you think they were pretty similar?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, they all lived about the same kind of a life. There wasn't

speaker: EM
words: much difference in 'em.

speaker: EM
words: "Liks

speaker: SS
words: Did the Greeks do a lot of drinking, too,^the Italians did?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. Italians, too-the wine all the time, you know. And

speaker: EM
words: the bootlegging-those Italians did a lot of bootlegging with

speaker: EM
words: wine-wine all the time, selling wine.

speaker: SS
words: Did either group mix much with the townspeople?

speaker: EM
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: Did either the Greeks or Italians mix much with the townspeople?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah. Whenever there was anything going on everybody mixed to

speaker: EM
words: gether, didn't make any difference whether you were a Greek or

speaker: EM
words: Italian or what you were. No, everybody was the same around here.

speaker: EM
words: They couldn't very well hold it against you what you were. Just

speaker: EM
words: like in World War I, why, my dad was a German and there was some

speaker: EM
words: Canadians here. Well, they, of course, they come down here and

speaker: EM
words: they'd stand around and they wouldn't work or anything else and

speaker: EM
words: they hollered at my dad one time, tried to stop him downtown and

speaker: EM
words: they were standing yp agsinst the hotel down there, that building

speaker: EM
words: and they stopped him and told him to get out of town, that he

speaker: EM
words: was a German and that. He was just a little guy only five foot

speaker: EM
words: tall and they told him to get out of town, he told 'em, says,

speaker: EM
words: "I don't have to go any place." And says, "Why don't you go back

speaker: EM
words: to Canada? How come you're not fighting?" Says, "You're not even

speaker: EM
words: working down here, standing around; waiting for people to feed

speaker: EM
words: you?" Of course, my dad, heck, he bought bonds and everything

speaker: EM
words: else, you know and that, nobody ever said anything to him. And

speaker: EM
words: he had a garden, grew a squash, oh, a great big thing-like thatand

speaker: EM
words: he gave that to the Red Cross and they raffled that off and

speaker: EM
words: got $500 out of it.

speaker: SS
words: Out of a squash?

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh. People bid on it, you know, different ones and they got

speaker: SS
words: $500 on that in donating. People come in, they'd bid, well, now

speaker: SS
words: say you went in, "I'll give $5." Alright, you give that $5-

speaker: SS
words: that was adonation-your bid and somebody else'd bid-"Well, I'll

speaker: SS
words: give six or seven. Well, whatever they could, they bid on it,

speaker: SS
words: whatever they bid is the amount they'd have to donate, see. And

speaker: SS
words: they got $500 out of it that way-out of that squash. But, heck,

speaker: SS
words: people never come around said, "You're German." And all this and

speaker: SS
words: that, they never bothered him at all.

speaker: SS
words: Iknow someplaces there was alot of suspicion, just because you

speaker: SS
words: were German.

speaker: EM
words: Heck, my dad worked down there and they never said a word to him.

speaker: EM
words: He never bothered them and they didn't bother him. When they as

speaker: EM
words: ked for donations or anything he give it to 'em and bought bonds.

speaker: EM
words: And that's a lot more than a lot of people did in this town-

speaker: EM
words: Americans. Just like those Canadians, they come over here across

speaker: EM
words: the line so they wouldn't have to go into the service, see.

speaker: EM
words: And after the war, why, they went back into Canada again. 'Course,

speaker: EM
words: they come in here because they had relatives here, see, and they

speaker: EM
words: said that they were just visiting here. Well, they stayed here

speaker: EM
words: and anything to keep from going into the Canadian service.

speaker: EM
words: Did most of the Greeks and Italians stay here right through the

speaker: EM
words: First World War?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, just like Gus over here and the others. A lot

speaker: EM
words: of 'em have left here now. Some of 'em went to Lewis ton, some

speaker: EM
words: to Spokane and a lot of 'em are dead now. Yeah, there's a lot

speaker: EM
words: of those old-timers-some of 'em still down at Lewiston, some are

speaker: EM
words: in Spokane-all over. Gus is the only one that's left around here.

speaker: SS
words: What about the IWWs? What do you know about them?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, I didn't really know them-what they were or anything. They

speaker: SS
words: were a bunch of loggers, that's all, guys that worked in the

speaker: SS
words: woods, lumberjacks, that's all. All they were after was better

speaker: SS
words: times, better food and lodging. Where they had these old bunkhouses

speaker: SS
words: and they'd give 'em little thin mattresses or something

speaker: SS
words: to lay on and they wanted better bunks and better mattresses

speaker: SS
words: and stuff like that, you know, and better wages, better food,

speaker: SS
words: they're the ones that built these camps up, what they are today.

speaker: SS
words: If it hadn't been for the IWWs they'd a never got it. And a lot

speaker: SS
words: of people think-talk about 'em being communists and all this and

speaker: SS
words: that, hell, those people, they don't even know what the IWWs were.

speaker: SS
words: All they know is just what they heard, see. But to be around 'em

speaker: SS
words: and know what they were, why, there's a lot of difference. Of

speaker: SS
words: course, there's a lot of people that just because the IWWs were

speaker: SS
words: in the camps and lumberjacks, why, they were called communists

speaker: SS
words: any everything else by certain people, you know. They'd go around

speaker: SS
words: and spread that old stuff.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the company had much to do with the propaganda against

speaker: SS
words: them?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. Naturally, because they didn't want to give in to the

speaker: EM
words: demands for that. The companies aren't going to give you any

speaker: EM
words: more than what they have to.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have much to do with the eight-hour day? The IWWs?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah they're the ones that done away with that tenhour

speaker: EM
words: law more than anybody else. Yeah, they were after the eighthour.

speaker: EM
words: They're the ones that really started these unions getting

speaker: EM
words: organized. That's the way your-Independent Workers of the World

speaker: EM
words: they called 'em-and then the AF of L come in and then the CIO.

speaker: EM
words: Yeah, they were the ones that really organized-started these or

speaker: EM
words: ganizations.

speaker: SS
words: Did you hear about-in those days did they try to pin a lot of

speaker: SS
words: sabotage on them? I've heard that they tried-they said, people

speaker: SS
words: said, that they set fires in the fields.

speaker: EM
words: Oh, that was all propaganda stuff. They never went around and

speaker: EM
words: building fires and everything like that. All they did, stayed

speaker: EM
words: right there in the woods and they wouldn't go out and work un

speaker: EM
words: less they got their demands and stuff like that, that's all.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard though that they didn't get much in Potlatch, in the

speaker: SS
words: town, they never made much of an impact here.

speaker: EM
words: No. Course, as far as the mill was concerned they didn't worry

speaker: EM
words: about the mill, they were after the camps, the logging camps

speaker: EM
words: and that, that's what they were after; better times there. Be

speaker: EM
words: cause they were loggers, they weren't sawmill men. They were

speaker: EM
words: guys that worked in-they were loggers, that's what they were,

speaker: EM
words: see. Of course, naturally, when they didn't get something, go

speaker: EM
words: out for wages or something, naturally the guys working in the

speaker: EM
words: mills, naturally tried to back them up, see. Because if they

speaker: EM
words: got it the guys in the mill would get it, too. They tried to get

speaker: EM
words: the mills to go on strikes and that^ and some of 'em did and some

speaker: EM
words: of 'em wouldn't. But they never went on strikes very much, just

speaker: EM
words: the woods mostly. Because the mills couldn't run unless they

speaker: EM
words: got logs in here^ and that was up to those guys in the woods.

speaker: SS
words: Did they ever have to shut the mill down here at Poatlatch be

speaker: SS
words: cause of the strikes out in the woods?

speaker: EM
words: No. Naw, when they went on a strike, why-well, the woods'd call

speaker: EM
words: for strikes, naturally if they belonged to the union the saw

speaker: EM
words: mill would have to go out with the woods, see. Because they was

speaker: EM
words: all working together, the sawmill and the loggers, the woods

speaker: EM
words: men, they all worked together. If the mill goes out, the camps

speaker: EM
words: will go out, too, because, heck, wouldn't do no good for the

speaker: EM
words: sawmill to go out down here and the camps working, too. So

speaker: EM
words: when they go out they all go out at the same time.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have anything to do with that militia they had here du

speaker: SS
words: ring the war?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, I belonged to it, yeah, there was a bunch of us belonged to

speaker: EM
words: it here. But we didn't do anything. We were just around here

speaker: EM
words: in case any trouble started that way, you know. But there was

speaker: EM
words: no trouble around here. After they made the Japs get out of

speaker: EM
words: here, why, heck, there wasn't any trouble. 'Course, they'd send

speaker: EM
words: us down to Lewiston or someplace like that for training more

speaker: EM
words: than anything else. And we'd go down there and guard that

speaker: EM
words: bridge across going into Lewiston and put some of us on there

speaker: EM
words: walking back and forth on that bridge. Maybe two guys'd go

speaker: EM
words: and walk half way and the other two guys comeing the other way

speaker: EM
words: and we'd meet. Then we'd do that for maybe an hour or two and

speaker: EM
words: then four more guys'd come in and take our place and that's

speaker: EM
words: the way we'd do that.

speaker: SS
words: Was this Second World War or First World War?

speaker: EM
words: No, second.

speaker: SS
words: I'd heard something about in the First World War they had a

speaker: SS
words: Home Guard, too, didn't they?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: And I heard that one of the ideas of that was that if there was

speaker: SS
words: any trouble from the IWWs that they'd use the Home Guard against

speaker: SS
words: them.

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah, they did. They said that they'd call out the Militia

speaker: EM
words: and all this and that. They never did, though.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think most of the guys in the camps were IWWs?

speaker: EM
words: Yeah, most of 'em were. 'Course, during the First World War

speaker: EM
words: they had to try to keep these mills and everything going because

speaker: EM
words: they had to have that lumber, you know. They had to ship that

speaker: EM
words: over to France and all over to build-for buildings and everything

speaker: EM
words: else and shipyards-they had to have lumber for ships and that.

speaker: EM
words: They had to keep these mills and that going. But they never had

speaker: EM
words: any trouble here. They just kept right on aworking as they al

speaker: EM
words: ways did. And, if you didn't want to work, why, you had to go

speaker: EM
words: into the service. The Second World War, why, they come aroundand

speaker: EM
words: if the company didn't want you to work, didn't want you on

speaker: EM
words: the job, why, you had to go into the service. Now, like they put

speaker: EM
words: me in 4F 'cause I had a certain job to do down here, why, I had

speaker: EM
words: to do that or else go into the service, see. So, they come around

speaker: EM
words: and asked you now, "Do you want to go into the service or what?"

speaker: EM
words: "Well, don't make any difference." "Well, alright, we're going

speaker: EM
words: to put you in 4F because we need you here." So they put you in

speaker: EM
words: 4F. Thdey^come around-service men come around and say, "Well,

speaker: EM
words: you're going to go whether you want to or not." They just asked

speaker: EM
words: you if you wanted to go in, that was up to you, if you wanted to

speaker: EM
words: and a lot of times they didn't give you a chance, you just had to

speaker: EM
words: take what you were given. You work here or else we're going to

speaker: EM
words: put you in 4F, if you don't want to go in 4F you have to go into

speaker: EM
words: the service. So what're you going to do? Naw, they kept quite

speaker: EM
words: a few, most of the guys, old-timers, guys that had worked here

speaker: EM
words: for a long time, those are the ones they really kept. But if

speaker: EM
words: you were acertain age, why you got into the draft then. And,

speaker: EM
words: of course, you had to take an examination, too before they put

speaker: EM
words: you in 4F. 'Course, Iwas sick then, Ihad had an ulcer opera

speaker: EM
words: tion and that and they examined me and told me that, why, they

speaker: EM
words: put me in 4F then.

speaker: SS
words: Ed, do you think people could make much money working here in

speaker: SS
words: the early days-enough to save and really build up?

speaker: EM
words: Oh, yeah. There was a lot of 'em did it. Work here and save

speaker: EM
words: every nickle they had. Hell, but it didn't do 'era much good.

speaker: EM
words: Had a couple of *em here. One of 'em lived two houses down here,

speaker: EM
words: a fellow worked in the planer and I worked with him down there.

speaker: EM
words: And he was saving and saving and saving all the time and he got

speaker: EM
words: to be over seventy years old and they wanted to retire him. "No",

speaker: EM
words: says, "I'm not going to retire yet," he says, "I got to have

speaker: EM
words: $50,000 in the bank," he says, "then I'll retire." And he worked

speaker: EM
words: there til he was seventy-six or seventy-eight years old. Finally

speaker: EM
words: he quit. He retired. Says, "I got my $50,000, now, I'll retire."

speaker: EM
words: Well, he was quite a drinker and he got to drinking and that.

speaker: EM
words: And I'd go downtown, a lot of times I'd see him down there and

speaker: EM
words: he had an old Chevrolet car and he'd drive it down there, he was

speaker: EM
words: all right driving down there, but he'd stop at the Confectionery

speaker: EM
words: and go in there and drink and he'd get so drunk that he couldn't

speaker: EM
words: drive his car homeland he'd see me or I'd go downtown or else

speaker: EM
words: I'd be going downtown and his wife would holler at me,"If you

speaker: EM
words: see Henry down there, would you see-help him home?" And I said,

speaker: EM
words: "Yeah, I'll go down." And I'd go down and look in the Confection

speaker: EM
words: ery and he'd be in there; "Henry, your wife wants you." "Okay.

speaker: EM
words: You got to drive my car home and I'll go." So, I'd get him out

speaker: EM
words: to the car and drive him home. And he lasted about a month; drank

speaker: EM
words: himself to death. Yeah, soon as he retired, boy, just hit that

speaker: EM
words: bottle all the time. Go down there and drink and drink and drink

speaker: EM
words: down at the Confectionery and he'd bring a bottle home with him.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think most people just managed to keep their heads above

speaker: SS
words: water, I mean, just managed to get by?

speaker: EM
words: Well, most of 'em. Boy, when I first got married during the De

speaker: EM
words: pression I worked three days a week. Boy, you think that wasn't

speaker: EM
words: something! I guess you was darn lucky to get three days a week.

speaker: EM
words: Working for 18% cents an hour. And I had about $1,200-company

speaker: EM
words: store and they'd give us credit, you know, as long as they knew

speaker: EM
words: we was going to stay and work and that, they give us credit. I

speaker: EM
words: was about $1,200 in the hole in the store from groceries and stuff

speaker: EM
words: you know. And, of course, after things picked up, why, you pay

speaker: EM
words: so much a month til you got your bill paid. But they always

speaker: EM
words: gave us credit. The company always give us credit.

speaker: SS
words: Was that for everybody or just for some people?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. Oh, there was a few-they let you go in debt for so much

speaker: SS
words: you know if you was working, but if you didn't work and they knew

speaker: SS
words: who to give to and that- guys they figured was going to stay here

speaker: SS
words: you know and that, they give you credit, but I knew a lot of 'em

speaker: SS
words: they wouldn't. Lot of people here, boy, that went on the county

speaker: SS
words: and everything else. Yeah, and then the wife she had to have an

speaker: SS
words: operation. And old Doc Gibson, I told him, "I don't know how I'm

speaker: SS
words: going to pay you." He says, "Well, pay what you can." And I'd

speaker: SS
words: get a couple of dollars or something and I'd go down there and

speaker: SS
words: give it to him; fine and dandy. And then my son-stepson there,

speaker: SS
words: he had to have an operation, appendix operation and Gibson opera

speaker: SS
words: ted on him. And I told him, I says, "I don't know how I'm going

speaker: SS
words: to make it." "Well," he says, "do what you can." So, I did and

speaker: SS
words: by golly, kept going on and on. One day I went in to make a pay

speaker: SS
words: ment, the woman there at the desk, why, she says, "Your bill is

speaker: SS
words: paid." I says, "What do you mean? My bill is paid." "Well," she

speaker: SS
words: said "that's it. I have the books here, your last payment says,

speaker: SS
words: "Paid in full."

speaker: SS
words: Old Gibson, by golly, he'd cancelled my bill

speaker: SS
words: right off. And he told me, he says, "Well, alot of the people

speaker: SS
words: came in there"and they was just ravin' because he wouldn't go

speaker: SS
words: out-one guy out here at Rock Creek-and he come in and Ihappened

speaker: SS
words: to be in the office here waiting for him and he come into Gibson

speaker: SS
words: and he says, "You got to come out and see my wife, she's sick."

speaker: SS
words: Gibson says, "I don't have to do anything." He says, "I don't

speaker: SS
words: have to go out and see your wife." "Well," he says, "you do,

speaker: SS
words: she's sick." He says, "Listen," he says, "you've owed a bill

speaker: SS
words: here and it's been going on for months and months," he says,

speaker: SS
words: "you never come in and even offer a dollar or anything," but,

speaker: SS
words: he says, "you go down there to the Confectionery and those beer

speaker: SS
words: parlors and set and drink beer," he says, "you can't come up

speaker: SS
words: here." He says, "If you see me downtown you hide around the

speaker: SS
words: block, go around the block to keep out of my way." Says, "I

speaker: SS
words: know, I see you." And this guys says, "Well, I'll sue you, if

speaker: SS
words: you don't go out there, if anything happens to my wife." He

speaker: SS
words: says, "Alright, have you got a lawyer?" The guy says, "No."

speaker: SS
words: Gibson says, "Here's a phone. I'll call a lawyer for you and you

speaker: SS
words: go ahead and sue me, will you." He says, "Now, there's the

speaker: SS
words: door there in the outer office," he says, "You get out and don't

speaker: SS
words: ever come back here again." He says, "Well, my wife dies,"

speaker: SS
words: says, "you'll be sued." He says, "Alright. There's other doc

speaker: SS
words: tors here, another one over in Moscow, go get one of them."

speaker: SS
words: Boy, that guy never did- I don't know who he ever got or anything

speaker: SS
words: but Gibson told me afterwards when I went in there in his office

speaker: SS
words: and he said, "Well, you heard all that." And I says, "Yeah."

speaker: SS
words: "Well," he says, "there's the difference. You come in and paid

speaker: SS
words: it a few dollars a month on your bills and that guy wouldn't

speaker: SS
words: even come in and offer a dollar." Says, "That's the difference."

speaker: SS
words: He says, "If people show me that they are willing to pay," he

speaker: SS
words: says, "I'll do anything for them," but he says-
